content:
6 min read
3 hours ago

--

I kept coming across the term “Progressive Web App” in blog posts, conference talks, and documentation. At some point, I realized I knew what a PWA could do, but I had no idea how it actually worked. So I decided to dive in. I assumed it would be a quick topic — read a couple of blogs, watch a YouTube video, and move on. Instead, I found myself diving into Service Workers, caching strategies, IndexedDB, and offline-first architecture. Somewhere along the way, PWAs stopped feeling like a buzzword and started making a lot of sense.
If you’ve heard terms like Service Worker, Web App Manifest, or Workbox and felt overwhelmed, this article is for you. I’m not going to quote documentation or list every browser API. Instead, I’ll share what finally made these concepts click for me.

The misconception I had
Like many people, I assumed a Progressive Web App was simply a website that could be installed on a phone.
Technically, that’s true — but it’s also a huge oversimplification.
The ability to “Install App” is probably the least interesting thing about a PWA.
What actually makes a PWA powerful is that it can continue working even when the internet doesn’t.
Think about opening Spotify on a flight or reading previously loaded articles when your network disappears. That’s the kind of experience PWAs aim to provide on the web.
At its core, a PWA is just a regular web application enhanced with a few browser capabilities that make it faster, more reliable, and much closer to a native app experience.

Traditional Website vs Progressive Web App
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A Service Worker sits between your application and the network, giving it control over how requests are handled.
The three pieces that make everything work
When I first started reading about PWAs, every tutorial mentioned three things: HTTPS, the Web App Manifest, and Service Workers.
Initially, they felt like completely separate concepts.
Eventually, I realized they’re simply three parts of the same system.
HTTPS is the easiest one to understand. Browsers don’t allow Service Workers to run on insecure websites because they have access to powerful APIs that intercept network requests. In other words, HTTPS isn’t really a feature — it’s a security requirement.
The Web App Manifest is much less mysterious than its name suggests. It’s just a JSON file that tells the browser how your application should look after installation. It contains your application’s name, icon, colors, and whether it should open inside the browser or in its own standalone window. Once it’s configured, you rarely think about it again.
The real star of the show is the Service Worker.

The moment Service Workers finally made sense
This was the concept that confused me the most.
I kept reading definitions like:
“A Service Worker is a script that runs in the background.”
Okay… but why?
The explanation that finally clicked was surprisingly simple.
Imagine every request your application makes — loading an image, fetching an API response, downloading JavaScript files.
Normally, those requests go directly to the server.
A Service Worker quietly steps in between.
Instead of immediately contacting the internet, the browser asks the Service Worker first.
The Service Worker can respond with a cached file, fetch fresh data from the server, combine both approaches, or even return an offline page if there’s no network available.
Once I started thinking of it as a smart traffic controller instead of some magical background script, everything became much easier to understand.

Service Worker Request Flow
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every network request passes through the Service Worker before reaching the server.
Caching isn’t just about making things faster
Before learning PWAs, I thought caching existed purely for performance.
Load a page once.
Store the files.
Reuse them later.
That’s true, but it’s only half the story.
Caching is also what allows an application to survive poor or nonexistent internet connections.
Of course, not every file should be treated the same way.
A logo almost never changes, so it makes sense to always load it from the cache. News articles, on the other hand, should usually come from the network because they change frequently.
That’s why different caching strategies exist.
Rather than memorizing their definitions, I found it easier to think about when I’d actually use them.
If I want maximum speed, I reach for Cache First.
If fresh data matters more than speed, Network First is usually the better choice.
And when I want both, Stale While Revalidate gives users an immediate response while quietly updating the cache in the background.
Those three strategies cover most real-world applications.

Cache Strategy Comparison
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Different types of data require different caching strategies
Offline applications need somewhere to store data
Caching static assets solves only part of the problem.
Imagine you’re building a notes application.
Or a task manager.
Or even a messaging app.
Where does the user’s actual data go when they’re offline?
This is where I discovered IndexedDB.
My first experience with the native IndexedDB API wasn’t particularly enjoyable. It felt verbose, event-driven, and much lower level than modern JavaScript.
Fortunately, I came across Dexie.js.
Dexie wraps IndexedDB with a much cleaner API, making it feel like working with a regular database instead of wrestling with browser events.
That one library made IndexedDB significantly less intimidating.

React → Dexie → IndexedDB Architecture
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Dexie provides a much cleaner interface while still using IndexedDB underneath.
Building a small project changed everything
Reading documentation helped.
Building something helped much more.
To put everything into practice, I built a simple news application using React, Vite, Workbox, and IndexedDB.
Whenever the application fetched articles from an API, it also stored them locally using Dexie.
Meanwhile, the Service Worker cached the application’s static assets.
The fun part came later.
I opened Chrome DevTools, switched the network to Offline, refreshed the page…
…and the application still loaded.
The articles I’d already viewed were still available.
The interface still worked.
Even navigation felt normal.
It wasn’t pretending to support offline mode — it actually did.
That was probably the moment PWAs stopped being a theory and became something tangible.
Offline-First News App
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Static assets are cached, article data is stored in IndexedDB, and the Service Worker decides where each request should go.The complete source code for the project is available on GitHub if you’d like to explore the implementation or build on top of it: https://github.com/Dumbledorithm/PWA-news-fetch

Are PWAs replacing native apps?
Probably not.
If you’re building mobile games, AR applications, or software that needs deep access to device hardware, native development is still the better choice.
But for dashboards, e-commerce platforms, booking systems, blogs, productivity tools, CRMs, and many internal business applications, PWAs are surprisingly capable.
You get one codebase that runs across devices, loads quickly, works offline, and doesn’t require users to visit an app store.
That’s a compelling trade-off.

Final thoughts
Before this week, if someone had asked me what a Progressive Web App was, I probably would have answered, “It’s a website you can install.”
Now I’d describe it differently.
A PWA is a web application that’s designed to keep working — even when the network doesn’t.
The technology itself isn’t magic. It’s simply a collection of well-designed browser APIs working together: Service Workers managing requests, caching strategies improving reliability, IndexedDB storing data locally, and tools like Workbox making the entire process much easier to implement.
I’m still learning, but one thing has already changed.
I no longer think of the web as something that’s always online.
Modern web applications don’t have to stop working just because the internet does.

In my next article, I’ll build an offline-first Progressive Web App from scratch using React, Vite, Workbox, IndexedDB, and Dexie.js, showing exactly how all these pieces fit together in code.
